notes: A minimum of four weeks of intensive ethnographic/historical research must be completed by the end of the fall semester of the senior year. This research can be done via an approved study abroad program, ANT35-524 (Racial History Project), or an independently developed project.

ANT35-424 (Theory and Method in Cultural Anthropology) is offered fall only and is restricted to declared majors.
ANT35-964 (Senior Seminar) is offered spring only and requires permission of instructor; the research period must be completed prior to taking this course.

notes: Optional senior art exhibition: BA students may seek approval for a senior art exhibition – see the professor in the focus area and the department chair for requirements. Students who are approved must take ART68-512 Exhibition Practicum in the semester of their senior exhibition.

electiveGroups.name: Four courses from the following Studio Art electives

notes: The Bachelor of Fine Arts (BFA) degree offers a more immersive experience, culminating in a professional art exhibition showcasing a unique creative vision.
To be admitted to the BFA degree program, students must submit a formal application and pass a portfolio jury during the sophomore or junior year.
BFA students must maintain a B- (2.67) grade point average and pass regular reviews by the studio faculty as a whole to continue in the program.

The total number of courses explicitly listed for the BFA is 16, however, the degree description states a requirement of '19 courses'. Total credits are calculated based on the stated '19 courses' at 4 credits per course.

All majors must successfully pass the Major Field Test in Mathematics or Computer Science.
A minimum grade of C- must be earned in any course if it is to count as a prerequisite for a subsequent Mathematics or Computer Science course.

Any three-credit or four-credit course at the 300 level or above will serve as an approved course unless the department explicitly indicates otherwise.
Credit for Honors (MAT52-984 or CSC54-984) will be counted, at most, once as an approved upper-level elective.

The B.S. in Computational Mathematics requires 2 additional approved upper-level electives and 4 supporting science courses, whereas the B.A. requires 1 additional approved upper-level elective and no supporting science courses.

MAT 52-164 Modern Calculus I must be completed no later than the sophomore year.
Two additional approved upper-level CS courses should normally be begun no later than junior year.

Residency Requirement: At least 12 credits at SU
Minimum of 56 credits total for the major.
The major consists of two blocks:
Block 1: 28 credits from one primary subject area. At least 20 of these credits must be above the introductory level.
Block 2: 28 additional credits from other subject areas. At least 20 of these credits must be above the introductory level.
Courses used to satisfy Block 1 may not be double-counted.
The program must be designed in consultation with the chairs of the departments/programs involved.
The program must be endorsed by a vote of the department/program in which the Block 1 subject area falls.
Final approval is required from the Curriculum Committee.
A high-impact capstone project is required, with its format dictated by the requirements of the Block 1 subject area.
The Independent Major replaces both a regularly offered major and a minor; no separate minor is required.

The definition of 'above introductory level' is not explicitly defined in the source documents. It is generally understood that courses numbered 0-19 are typically introductory and 20-89 are upper-level, but this should be confirmed with an advisor and the Curriculum Committee.

electiveGroups.name: Language Courses (200-level or above)
notes: Three language courses beyond the general education requirement of three semesters (or equivalent placement).
Options: a) Three 200+ level courses in the same language, OR b) Two 200+ level courses in one language AND one course in a second language spoken at the study abroad location.

electiveGroups.name: Track Courses (choose one track)
notes: Students select ONE track: Global Politics and Economy, Global Society and Culture, or Global Justice.
No more than 2 track courses may be from a single discipline.

Global Justice track courses are those from Global Politics and Economy or Global Society and Culture that carry a Social Justice (SJ) tag. SJ-tagged courses identified in the source include: ANT35-244, ANT35-334, ARH71-264, HIS16-264, HIS16-314, HIS16-434, PHI18-284, PSC32-184, REL19-204, REL19-274, REL19-284, REL19-324, REL19-344, REL19-384.
Track assignment for other courses (Global Politics and Economy vs. Global Society and Culture) must be confirmed with the International Studies Committee or the approved course list.
Courses not listed under the tracks may be approved on a case-by-case basis by the International Studies Committee.
Language proficiency exam required before graduation.
American Sign Language (ASL) may NOT be used to fulfill the language requirement.

Mandatory study abroad: minimum one full semester OR one summer (at least 6 weeks) in a location where English is not the dominant language. Strongly recommended that programs are conducted primarily in a language other than English.
Maximum 3 study abroad (or transfer) courses may count toward the IS major.
Capstone (INS38-894) must be taken after study abroad and most track courses; project must relate to student’s selected track.
Students planning to follow the Global Politics and Economy track should pay close attention to the pre-requisites for courses in Economics, Political Science, and especially Business.

electiveGroups.name: Track Courses
notes: Three courses from International Studies tracks (Global Politics and Economy, Global Society and Culture, or Global Justice).

The Global Justice track consists of courses from either other track that carry a Social Justice (SJ) tag. SJ-tagged courses identified in the source include: ANT35-244, ANT35-334, ARH71-264, HIS16-264, HIS16-314, HIS16-434, PHI18-284, PSC32-184, REL19-204, REL19-274, REL19-284, REL19-324, REL19-344, REL19-384.
Track assignment for other courses (Global Politics and Economy vs. Global Society and Culture) must be confirmed with the International Studies Committee or the approved course list.

electiveGroups.name: Additional Language or Track Course
notes: One semester of foreign language beyond the general education requirement of three semesters (200-level or above); or, if a student has tested out of this language requirement, a fifth course from any of the International Studies tracks.
At least 3 of the 5 courses in the minor must be taken at the upper-level.
No more than 2 of the 5 courses may be from the same discipline (for the entire minor).
Study abroad is encouraged but NOT required.
The International Studies minor is an official Paideia Minor and counts toward Paideia Distinction.
No introductory/lower-level Latin courses count toward the 7 upper-level electives.
Teacher certification in Latin is available — see Education Department catalog for requirements.
No capstone required for the minor.
Up to 2 of the 5 courses may be lower-level Latin.

This degree is intended for students pursuing a career as a music educator in elementary, middle or high schools.
Both instrumental and vocal tracks are offered.
Four tracks available: Music Education with/without Certification (choral/vocal emphasis), and Music Education with/without Certification (instrumental emphasis).
Certification requires: Admission to the Teacher Education Program (Education Department), completion of 37 credits of professional Education courses, and a satisfactory TExES exam score.
During their senior year, students pursuing certification will practice teach at schools in the Austin area.

electiveGroups.name: Sociology Electives
notes: Two additional courses in or contributing to sociology required.
At least one of these two courses must be upper-level (200-700 level).
One of these two courses may be an upper-level Anthropology course (exact course not specified in source).
Minimum of 30 credits (8 courses) for the major.
SOC34-314, SOC34-344, SOC34-394, and SOC34-964 must be taken at Southwestern and cannot be satisfied by transfer credit or other substitutions.
MAT52-114 Introduction to Statistics is a required supporting course for the major and is not counted among the eight sociology courses.
The cumulative sequence must be followed: SOC34-114 + MAT52-114 → SOC34-314 → SOC34-344 → SOC34-394 → SOC34-964.
SOC34-314 (Research Methods) is recommended sophomore year and must precede senior year.
SOC34-344 (Sociological Theory) is recommended junior year (may be taken sophomore year for study abroad flexibility) and must precede senior year.
SOC34-394 (Sociological Research Practicum) is to be taken in the spring of junior year.
SOC34-964 (Senior Research Seminar/Capstone) is to be taken in the fall of senior year; missing it requires waiting until the next fall semester.
Declared major or minor status is a prerequisite for SOC34-314 and SOC34-344.
Academic internship credits (900-level) do NOT count toward major requirements.
SOC34-394 Sociological Research Practicum credit hours not explicitly stated in source, assumed 4.

electiveGroups.name: Sociology Minor Electives
notes: Four additional courses in or contributing to Sociology required.

ANT35-104 Introduction to Cultural Anthropology can count toward the minor.
Minimum of 18 credits (5 courses) for the minor.
No capstone or research sequence is required for the minor.
No statistics prerequisite is imposed by the minor itself (though SOC34-314 and SOC34-344 have their own prerequisites if students choose those electives).
AKD (Alpha Kappa Delta) eligibility extends to declared minors (with appropriate GPA).
